disease.name: Obstructive Jaundice
disease.note: Jaundice due to impaired bile flow from the liver to the duodenum. Causes include gallstones (choledocholithiasis), benign strictures, and malignant tumors (pancreatic cancer, cholangiocarcinoma). Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with elevated ALP/GGT is the hallmark. Courvoisier's law: if the gallbladder is palpable and non-tender in a jaundiced patient, the cause is unlikely to be gallstones.
presentingComplaints: Yellow discoloration of the eyes and skin
Dark urine like strong tea or Coca-Cola
pathophysiologyDetail: Biliary obstruction: mechanical blockage of bile flow from liver to duodenum; causes include (1) gallstones impacted in CBD (choledocholithiasis — most common), (2) malignant strictures (pancreatic head cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, ampullary tumor), (3) benign strictures (post-cholecystectomy, PSC, chronic pancreatitis), (4) parasitic (Ascaris, Clonorchis sinensis)

Choledocholithiasis: gallstones form in gallbladder → migrate through cystic duct → become impacted in CBD; stones lodge at (1) ampulla of Vater (most common — causes both biliary and pancreatic duct obstruction), (2) distal CBD, (3) proximal CBD; intermittent obstruction causes fluctuating jaundice (ball-valve effect)

Malignant obstructive jaundice: pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (60-70% of malignant cases), cholangiocarcinoma (20-30%), ampullary carcinoma, gallbladder cancer, metastatic nodes at porta hepatis; characterized by progressive, painless jaundice with weight loss; Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder) in distal CBD obstruction

Biliary pressure dynamics: normal CBD pressure 5-10 cm H2O; after obstruction pressure rises to 15-25 cm H2O within hours; hepatic bile secretion continues against resistance; intrahepatic bile ducts dilate proximally; prolonged obstruction (>30 days) → secondary biliary cirrhosis, hepatic fibrosis

Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia: bilirubin diglucuronide (conjugated) accumulates in blood because it cannot be excreted; appears in urine (dark urine) because conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble and filtered by kidneys; absence of urobilinogen (no bilirubin reaches gut for conversion by gut bacteria)

Vitamin K malabsorption: bile salts are essential for absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K); vitamin K deficiency → impaired synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X → prolonged PT/INR; corrects with parenteral vitamin K (distinguishes from hepatocellular disease where PT does not correct)

Pruritus mechanism: bile salts (especially lithocholate) and bilirubin deposition in skin → stimulate nerve endings → intense itching; worst on palms, soles, and under tight clothing; antihistamines often ineffective; cholestyramine (bile acid sequestrant) more effective; rifampicin reduces pruritus

Endotoxemia and renal dysfunction: absence of bile salts in gut → loss of barrier function → endotoxin (LPS) absorption into portal circulation → systemic endotoxemia → TNF-α, IL-6 mediated renal vasoconstriction → hepatorenal syndrome; exacerbated by volume depletion, nephrotoxic agents

Cholangitis pathophysiology: biliary obstruction + bacterial contamination → ascending cholangitis; organisms (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, anaerobes) proliferate in stagnant bile → increased biliary pressure → cholangiovenous reflux → systemic bacteremia/sepsis; Charcot triad (fever, RUQ pain, jaundice), Reynolds pentad (+ hypotension, altered mental status)

Benign biliary stricture causes: iatrogenic (laparoscopic cholecystectomy — most common cause of benign stricture), anastomotic (post-liver transplant, post-hepaticojejunostomy), inflammatory (chronic pancreatitis, PSC, IgG4-related disease), traumatic; smooth, tapered stricture on imaging; may present months to years after initial injury

East African context: gallstone-related obstruction is increasing with dietary transition (higher fat intake, rising obesity); malignant obstruction from pancreatic cancer is less common than in Western populations but increasing; Ascaris lumbricoides biliary infestation causes obstruction in areas with poor sanitation; ERCP availability limited to 5-6 centers in Kenya (Nairobi, Mombasa, Kisumu); many patients rely on surgical CBD exploration

Parasitic biliary obstruction: Ascaris lumbricoides migrates from duodenum into CBD → biliary colic, obstructive jaundice, cholangitis, pancreatitis; worms visible on US as tubular echogenic structures in CBD; ERCP for extraction; liver flukes (Clonorchis sinensis, Opisthorchis viverrini) cause biliary epithelial hyperplasia, cholangiocarcinoma risk in endemic areas

HIV cholangiopathy: AIDS cholangiopathy causes biliary strictures (papillary stenosis, sclerosing cholangitis-like changes) from opportunistic infections (CMV, Cryptosporidium, Microsporidia); presents with RUQ pain, elevated ALP, dilated bile ducts; ERCP with sphincterotomy for papillary stenosis; treat underlying infection

landmarkTrials.trial: Early vs Delayed ERCP in Acute Cholangitis (Tokyo Guidelines 2018 validation — multicenter)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with acute cholangitis and biliary obstruction
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early ERCP (<24h) vs delayed ERCP (>24-48h) for biliary drainage
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early ERCP: lower mortality (3% vs 10%), shorter hospital stay (7 vs 12 days), fewer organ failures (5% vs 15%). Recommended within 24h for moderate-severe cholangitis. Mild cholangitis may undergo elective ERCP within 48-72h after antibiotics.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: ERCP access in Kenya is limited; early referral to ERCP-capable center is critical

landmarkTrials.trial: Preoperative Biliary Drainage for Malignant Obstructive Jaundice (2004, The Lancet — Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of randomized trials comparing pre-op biliary drainage vs early surgery for malignant jaundice
landmarkTrials.intervention: Pre-operative ERCP/PTC drainage with stent vs direct surgical resection without drainage
landmarkTrials.outcome: Routine pre-op drainage: higher complication rate (drainage-related pancreatitis, cholangitis, stent occlusion) without mortality benefit. Selective use recommended when: bilirubin >15 mg/dL, cholangitis present, delayed surgery planned, or for borderline resectable tumors during neoadjuvant therapy.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Pre-op drainage reserved for bilirubin >250 µmol/L or cholangitis in Kenyan practice

landmarkTrials.trial: Plastic vs Metal Stents for Malignant Biliary Obstruction (2010, Gastrointestinal Endoscopy — Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: RCTs comparing plastic vs metal stents for malignant distal biliary obstruction
landmarkTrials.intervention: Plastic stent (10 Fr) vs self-expanding metal stent (SEMS) for malignant obstructive jaundice
landmarkTrials.outcome: Metal stents: longer patency (6-12 months vs 3-4 months for plastic), fewer re-interventions (20% vs 50% at 6 months), better quality of life. Cost-effective if life expectancy >3-4 months. Plastic stents acceptable for short-term palliation (<3 months) or diagnostic uncertainty.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Metal stents preferred for malignant disease with expected survival >3 months; plastic stents cheaper and available

landmarkTrials.trial: ERCP vs PTC for Biliary Drainage in Hilar Obstruction (2013, Hepatobiliary & Pancreatic Diseases International — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients with hilar biliary obstruction randomized to ERCP or PTC
landmarkTrials.intervention: ERCP with stent placement vs PTC with external-internal drainage
landmarkTrials.outcome: PTC: higher success for hilar drainage (95% vs 65% for ERCP, p<0.01). ERCP: lower complication rate (8% vs 20%). PTC preferred for hilar (Bismuth II-IV) obstruction. ERCP preferred for distal obstruction. Combined approach for complex hilar tumors.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: PTC is more available in Kenya than ERCP at district level; useful for hilar obstruction

landmarkTrials.trial: Preoperative Biliary Drainage vs Early Surgery in Pancreatic Head Cancer (2018, NEJM — SWOG S1505)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with resectable pancreatic head cancer and jaundice
landmarkTrials.intervention: Preoperative biliary drainage with stent vs early surgery within 1-2 weeks
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in major complications (39% vs 41%), mortality (3% vs 4%), or survival between groups. Early surgery avoids stent-related complications (pancreatitis 5%, cholangitis 8%). Drainage indicated only if bilirubin >250 or surgery delayed >2 weeks.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Early surgery preferred in Kenya to avoid ERCP delay and stent costs

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotic Prophylaxis for ERCP (2007, Cochrane Review — Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: RCTs of antibiotic prophylaxis before ERCP for biliary obstruction
landmarkTrials.intervention: Prophylactic antibiotics before ERCP vs no prophylaxis
landmarkTrials.outcome: Antibiotic prophylaxis reduced cholangitis after ERCP from 5.5% to 2.5% (RR 0.4). Greatest benefit when incomplete drainage is anticipated. Routine prophylaxis not needed for all cases; recommended when incomplete drainage likely or in immunocompromised patients.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Prophylactic antibiotics given routinely in Kenya before ERCP given higher background infection rates

landmarkTrials.trial: Gallstone Ileus Management — Enterolithotomy vs One-Stage (2010, World Journal of Surgery — Multicenter)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with gallstone ileus (gallstone obstruction of distal ileum)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Enterolithotomy alone vs enterolithotomy + cholecystectomy + fistula closure (one-stage)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Enterolithotomy alone: lower morbidity (30% vs 55%) and mortality (5% vs 20%) than one-stage. Avoid one-stage in elderly, unstable patients. Interval cholecystectomy after recovery, though fistula may close spontaneously. One-stage reserved for young, fit patients.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Gallstone ileus seen in elderly with large gallstones; simple enterolithotomy preferred in Kenyan setting

evidenceTable.therapy: Early vs delayed ERCP for cholangitis
Pre-op biliary drainage for malignant jaundice (Meta-analysis)
Metal vs plastic stent for malignant obstruction
evidenceTable.effect: Patency 6-12 mo vs 3-4 mo; re-intervention 20% vs 50%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Metal stent if life expectancy >3 months

evidenceTable.therapy: ERCP vs PTC for hilar obstruction (RCT)
evidenceTable.effect: Success PTC 95% vs ERCP 65%; complications PTC 20% vs ERCP 8%
evidenceTable.recommendation: PTC first for hilar; ERCP for distal obstruction

evidenceTable.therapy: Pre-op drainage vs early surgery in pancreatic cancer (RCT)
evidenceTable.effect: No significant difference; drainage adds pancreatitis 5%, cholangitis 8%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Early surgery preferred; drainage only if bilirubin >15
Prophylaxis when incomplete drainage anticipated or immunocompromised

evidenceTable.therapy: Vitamin K for coagulopathy in obstructive jaundice
evidenceTable.effect: Vitamin K 10 mg IV corrects INR within 24-48h in majority
evidenceTable.recommendation: Vitamin K 10 mg IV daily × 3 days pre-procedure

evidenceTable.therapy: Cholestyramine for pruritus in obstructive jaundice
evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line for pruritus; start 4g PO QID

evidenceTable.therapy: Sphincterotomy + stone extraction vs open CBD exploration for choledocholithiasis
evidenceTable.effect: Success 90% ERCP vs 95% open; LOS 3 vs 10 days; lower morbidity ERCP
evidenceTable.recommendation: ERCP first-line; open surgery when ERCP unavailable or failed

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Confirm obstructive nature — history (dark urine, pale stools, pruritus), examination (jaundice, excoriations, Courvoisier sign), LFTs (conjugated >50%, ALP/GGT disproportionately elevated vs ALT/AST)

Step 2: Abdominal ultrasound — first-line imaging: bile duct dilation (CBD >6 mm, intrahepatic ducts), level of obstruction, gallstones, CBD stones, mass, Courvoisier gallbladder. Sensitivity 70-80% for detecting obstruction

Step 3: Differentiate stone vs malignant — clinical: fluctuating jaundice + colicky pain suggests stone; progressive painless jaundice + weight loss suggests malignancy. MRC for definitive stone detection and ductal anatomy

Step 4: MRCP — gold standard non-invasive: sensitivity 95% for CBD stones, 90% for malignant strictures. Double duct sign (dilated CBD + pancreatic duct) = pancreatic head mass until proven otherwise. Smooth tapering stricture = benign; irregular shouldered stricture = malignant

Step 5: CT abdomen (pancreatic protocol) — triple-phase: detect pancreatic mass, assess resectability, vascular involvement (SMA, SMV, celiac, portal vein), distant metastases, lymphadenopathy. Essential if malignancy suspected

Step 6: EUS ± FNA — for tissue diagnosis of suspected malignancy, detection of small CBD stones (<5 mm) not seen on MRCP, assessment of tumor depth and nodal involvement; FNA cytology for tissue confirmation before neoadjuvant therapy

Step 7: ERCP — therapeutic: sphincterotomy + stone extraction for choledocholithiasis; stent placement for malignant/biliary stricture drainage; brushings for cytology if cholangiocarcinoma suspected. Combined diagnostic and therapeutic. Avoid diagnostic-only ERCP

Step 8: PTC — percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography with external/internal drainage: for hilar obstruction (Bismuth II-IV), failed ERCP, proximal bile duct access needed, internalization of drain possible after tract maturation
Step 9: Coagulation assessment — PT/INR before any invasive procedure; if prolonged: Vitamin K 10 mg IV daily × 3 days; if still prolonged and urgent procedure: FFP 15 mL/kg

Kenya adaptation: US is widely available first-line; MRCP at major centers; ERCP at 5-6 centers; PTC more widely available (interventional radiology at provincial hospitals); CT pancreas protocol at major referral hospitals; surgical CBD exploration is still common when ERCP unavailable
managementPearls: Do NOT operate on a jaundiced patient with uncorrected coagulopathy — Vitamin K 10 mg IV daily for 3 days will correct PT in biliary obstruction (vs hepatocellular disease where PT does not correct); check INR before any procedure

Courvoisier's law: 'If the gallbladder is palpable in a jaundiced patient, the cause is unlikely to be gallstones' — a small, fibrotic gallbladder from chronic cholecystitis cannot distend even if obstructed; a palpable gallbladder suggests malignant distal obstruction

For gallstone-related obstructive jaundice, perform ERCP + sphincterotomy + stone extraction first, then laparoscopic cholecystectomy — ideally in the same admission (within 72 hours of ERCP) to reduce risk of recurrent events while awaiting surgery

Fluctuating jaundice suggests a stone causing intermittent obstruction (ball-valve effect), not malignancy. Serial LFTs showing bilirubin rising and falling are characteristic of choledocholithiasis. Progressive relentless rise suggests malignant obstruction

Pre-operative biliary drainage for malignant jaundice is controversial — current evidence does NOT support routine drainage; it increases complication rates (pancreatitis, cholangitis, stent occlusion). Drain only if: bilirubin >15 mg/dL (>250 µmol/L), cholangitis present, or surgery will be delayed >2 weeks

For hilar cholangiocarcinoma (Klatskin tumor), drainage should be planned carefully — drain the future liver remnant (FLR), not the lobe to be resected; bilateral drainage rarely needed if one lobe provides adequate functional liver; unnecessary drainage increases cholangitis risk

Pruritus management algorithm: first-line cholestyramine 4 g PO QID (tastes unpleasant, mix with juice); second-line rifampicin 150-300 mg PO BID (hepatotoxic — monitor LFTs); third-line naltrexone 25-50 mg PO daily (opioid antagonist). Effective biliary drainage is the definitive treatment

Post-ERCP pancreatitis prevention: rectal indomethacin 100 mg immediately after ERCP for all patients; pancreatic duct stent (5 Fr, short) for high-risk patients (difficult cannulation, precut sphincterotomy, young female, Sphincter of Oddi dysfunction); aggressive periprocedural IV fluids (Ringer's lactate 3 mL/kg/h)

In patients with biliary obstruction and cholangitis, biliary drainage is THE priority — antibiotics alone will not resolve cholangitis without relief of obstruction; the single most important intervention is ERCP with drainage within 24 hours for moderate-severe cholangitis (Tokyo Guidelines 2018 Grade II-III)

Gallstone ileus: mechanical obstruction from a large gallstone (usually >2.5 cm) that erodes through a cholecystoduodenal fistula and impacts at the ileocecal valve; Rigler triad on XR: pneumobilia, ectopic gallstone, small bowel obstruction; treatment is enterolithotomy alone in elderly; one-stage (enterolithotomy + cholecystectomy + fistula closure) only in young fit patients

Mirizzi syndrome: a gallstone impacted in the cystic duct or gallbladder neck that compresses the common hepatic duct, causing jaundice; Type I: external compression; Types II-V: erosion into CHD (cholecystocholedochal fistula); requires subtotal cholecystectomy with bile duct repair/precautions; open approach preferred; ERCP stent pre-op for complex cases

In Kenyan district hospitals where ERCP is unavailable, open CBD exploration with supraduodenal choledochotomy, stone extraction, and T-tube placement is the standard approach for choledocholithiasis; patient should be referred for ERCP post-op if needed for retained stones
operativeNuances: ERCP cannulation: 'keep the wire in the duct' — once the bile duct is cannulated, maintain wire access throughout; use a guidewire to assist with cannulation (wire-guided cannulation reduces post-ERCP pancreatitis vs contrast injection); avoid excessive pancreatic duct manipulation

Sphincterotomy technique: use a bow-type sphincterotome; position at 11-12 o'clock direction (away from pancreatic orifice at 1-2 o'clock); cut in controlled increments using 'lift and cut' technique; maximal safe cut length is the transverse fold of the papilla (approximately 1-1.5 cm); avoid cutting beyond the fold

Stone extraction: stones <1 cm: Dormia basket or extraction balloon; stones 1-1.5 cm: sphincterotomy + balloon extraction (balloon sweeps above the stone, then inflated and withdrawn); stones >1.5 cm: mechanical lithotripsy (crush stone with lithotripter basket) or ESWL; large impacted stones may require a plastic stent as bridge to definitive therapy

Open CBD exploration: supraduodenal choledochotomy — Kocher maneuver to mobilize duodenum; two traction sutures on anterior CBD; longitudinal incision on anterior CBD 1-2 cm; explore proximally and distally with Desjardins forceps; flush with saline; complete choledochoscopy if available; T-tube placement with 12-14 Fr T-tube; cholangiogram via T-tube before closure

T-tube cholangiography: inject diluted contrast (50:50 with saline) under low pressure — should flow freely into duodenum; check for retained stones, distal patency; T-tube left for 10-14 days before removal; confirm tract maturation with T-tube cholangiogram before removal; persistent drainage >300 mL/day suggests distal obstruction

Hepaticojejunostomy (Roux-en-Y) for benign stricture: 50 cm Roux limb; single-layer mucosa-to-mucosa anastomosis using 4-0 or 5-0 absorbable suture; place biliary stent (8-10 Fr pediatric feeding tube) across anastomosis exiting through the Roux limb (transhepatically or through the closed end of the Roux limb); drain near anastomosis

For malignant hilar obstruction (Bismuth type): Type I (common hepatic duct) — single metal stent above bifurcation; Type II (involves bifurcation but not secondaries) — bilateral metal stents (side-by-side or Y-configuration) or unilateral drainage of FLR; Types III-IV — PTC drainage of the most accessible dilated duct; avoid draining both lobes if one is adequate

Laparoscopic CBD exploration: transcystic approach for small (<8 mm) distal stones; choledochotomy for larger/proximal stones; require choledochoscopy; technically demanding; similar outcomes to ERCP for CBD stones; single-stage treatment (LC + CBD exploration) avoids need for pre-op ERCP in many patients

In Mirizzi syndrome: do NOT attempt to dissect Calot's triangle early — the anatomy is distorted by inflammation and fibrosis; open approach strongly preferred; subtotal cholecystectomy (leave posterior wall adherent to liver/duct) with omental plug or direct repair of choledochal fistula; place T-tube or biliary stent across repair; refer to tertiary center if complex

prognosis.gallstoneObstruction: Excellent after ERCP + stone extraction + cholecystectomy; >95% resolution; recurrence risk <5% after cholecystectomy; recurrent stones if gallstones not removed (up to 30%)
prognosis.malignantObstructionResectable: 5-year survival 20-30% after Whipple for pancreatic cancer; 40-60% for distal cholangiocarcinoma; 20-40% for ampullary carcinoma; depends on lymph node status, margin status, and tumor biology
prognosis.malignantObstructionUnresectable: Median survival 6-12 months with biliary stent + chemotherapy; 3-6 months without treatment; quality of life improved with stenting; death from tumor progression, liver failure, cholangitis, cachexia
prognosis.benignBiliaryStricture: Good: 80-90% success with endoscopic balloon dilation + multiple stents over 12-24 months; surgery (hepaticojejunostomy) 90-95% success if endoscopic therapy fails; recurrence 10-15% over 5-10 years
prognosticFactors: Level of obstruction (distal better than hilar for access)
Duration of jaundice — prolonged >30 days → secondary biliary cirrhosis
Presence of cholangitis — increases mortality, especially if septic shock
Bilirubin level — >15 mg/dL associated with worse outcomes
CA 19-9 level — >1000 U/mL suggests unresectable, metastatic disease
Coagulopathy — uncorrectable PT suggests hepatocellular dysfunction (worse prognosis)
Patient age and comorbidities — elderly, diabetics, cirrhotics have higher mortality
Nutritional status — albumin <30 g/L predicts poor surgical outcomes
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Gallstone obstruction increasing with dietary shifts; malignant obstruction often presents late with advanced disease; limited ERCP access means surgical CBD exploration is common; post-cholecystectomy bile duct injuries are a significant cause of benign stricture in Kenya; referral to tertiary centers delayed by geography and cost

history_questions.question: When did you first notice yellow discoloration of your eyes?
Is your urine dark like tea or Coca-Cola?
Are your stools pale or clay-colored?
Do you have itching all over your body?
Do you have pain in your right upper abdomen?
Is the pain constant or intermittent? Colicky or dull?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past months?
Do you have known gallstones?
Have you had gallbladder surgery before?
Have you had fever with chills?
Do you have a history of pancreatitis?
Do you smoke cigarettes?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or beer?
Have you noticed a lump in your upper abdomen?
Do you have diabetes or has it started recently?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Do you bruise or bleed easily?
Are you on any medications or traditional herbs?
Has the jaundice been constant or does it come and go (fluctuating)?
Have you ever had surgery on your gallbladder or bile ducts?
Do you have a known history of hepatitis or chronic liver disease?
Have you noticed any lumps or swelling in your upper abdomen?
Have you been diagnosed with primary sclerosing cholangitis?
Do you have inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's or ulcerative colitis)?
Did you recently pass worms in your stool — roundworms or tapeworms?
Have you ever had an ERCP procedure before?
Do you take traditional herbal remedies for your symptoms?
Have you had weight loss of more than 5 kg in the last 3 months?
Do you have pain after eating fatty meals?
Have you noticed your abdomen becoming swollen or distended (ascites)?
Do you have easy bruising or bleeding from gums/nose?
Have you had blood transfusions in the past?
Do you have a family history of gallbladder disease or pancreatic cancer?
Do you work with chemicals or solvents that could affect your liver?
Have you travelled to areas where liver flukes are common (e.g. Lake Victoria region)?
Are you pregnant?

examination_findings.finding: Jaundice — yellow sclera and generalized skin icterus
Excoriations — scratch marks from pruritus (intense itching)
Right upper quadrant tenderness
Palpable gallbladder — Courvoisier sign (non-tender, distended, in malignant obstruction)
Hepatomegaly — enlarged liver
Murphy's sign — if cholecystitis also present
Fever — if cholangitis present
Tachycardia from sepsis or dehydration
Signs of coagulopathy — bruising, petechiae, bleeding from IV sites
Ascites (if malignant or chronic)
Palpable abdominal mass (if pancreatic or GB malignancy)
Supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow's) if metastatic
Muscle wasting and cachexia in malignant obstruction
Xanthomas (cholesterol deposits) in chronic cholestasis
Pale/clay-colored stools on digital rectal exam
Dark amber/tea-colored urine on visual inspection
Right upper quadrant mass — pancreatic head mass or cholangiocarcinoma
Caput medusae — dilated periumbilical veins (portal hypertension from chronic obstruction)
Spider angiomas on trunk/face — suggests chronic hepatocellular disease, not obstruction
Palmar erythema — may be present in chronic obstruction with cirrhosis
Asterixis (liver flap) — hepatic encephalopathy if acute deterioration
Signs of dehydration — dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor
Lymphadenopathy — supraclavicular (Virchow's node, left) if metastatic pancreatic cancer
Periumbilical nodule (Sister Mary Joseph nodule) — metastatic from GI malignancy
Bulging flanks and shifting dullness (ascites) — from peritoneal carcinomatosis or portal hypertension
Rectal shelf (Blumer's shelf) — peritoneal seeding on DRE from advanced malignancy
Signs of chronic liver disease: gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy, loss of body hair
Fetor hepaticus — sweet, musty breath odor in advanced liver disease
Tachycardia and hypotension — if cholangitis with septic shock
Right subcostal or epigastric tenderness with guarding
Murphy's sign — inspiratory arrest on deep RUQ palpation if gallstone-related

bedside_scores.name: Bilirubin Level + ALP Pattern
bedside_scores.interpretation: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia + high ALP/GGT = biliary obstruction until proven otherwise

riskFactors.note: Most common cause — stones migrate from GB to CBD
Head of pancreas tumors obstruct distal CBD
Klatskin tumor at hilum; PSC association
Bile duct injury or stricture formation
Autoimmune disease with progressive biliary fibrosis
Fibrosis of pancreatic head compresses distal CBD
Ascaris lumbricoides, liver flukes (Clonorchis) — endemic in parts of Africa

subtypes.name: Obstructive Jaundice from Choledocholithiasis
subtypes.management: ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy after ERCP (preferably same admission). If failed ERCP: laparoscopic CBD exploration or open CBD exploration.

subtypes.name: Obstructive Jaundice from Malignancy
subtypes.management: Staging CT/MRI. ERCP with biliary stent (plastic or metal) for palliation or pre-op drainage. Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) for resectable periampullary tumors. Distal pancreatectomy for body/tail. Palliative chemotherapy if unresectable.

subtypes.name: Benign Biliary Stricture
subtypes.management: ERCP with balloon dilatation and multiple plastic stents. Serial stent upsizing every 3 months for 12-24 months. Hepaticojejunostomy (Roux-en-Y) for failed endoscopic management or complex hilar strictures.

mimics.whyMimic: Jaundice, can have mild itching, RUQ discomfort
Jaundice, severe pruritus in pregnant woman
Chronic cholestasis, pruritus, jaundice, elevated ALP/GGT
Jaundice, RUQ pain, elevated bilirubin and LFTs
Jaundice, elevated ALP/GGT, fever if biliary source suspected
Episodic jaundice, pruritus, elevated bile acids
RUQ pain, jaundice, intermittent biliary obstruction
Obstructive jaundice, dilated ducts post-liver transplant
Jaundice, pruritus, elevated ALP after starting new medication

bedside.name: Urine dipstick
bedside.indication: Bilirubin positive + urobilinogen negative = obstructive jaundice

bedside.name: Digital Rectal Exam
bedside.indication: Pale stool on exam; rectal shelf if metastatic

laboratory.name: ALP, GGT
laboratory.indication: Cholestatic enzymes — markedly elevated in obstruction

laboratory.name: ALT, AST
laboratory.indication: Mild to moderate elevation in obstruction

laboratory.name: CEA
laboratory.indication: Non-specific; elevated in biliary and colon cancer

laboratory.name: INR / Coagulation profile
laboratory.indication: Vitamin K malabsorption — prolongs PT; correct with Vitamin K

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: Low in chronic liver disease, malnutrition, malignancy

laboratory.name: Urea, Creatinine, Electrolytes
laboratory.indication: Assess renal function — hepatorenal syndrome risk

laboratory.name: Blood cultures
laboratory.indication: If fever present — rule out cholangitis

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: First-line: dilated bile ducts, gallstones, CBD diameter, mass, Courvoisier GB

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: Gold standard non-invasive: stone level, stricture level, pancreatic duct

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol)
imaging.indication: Detect mass, assess tumor resectability, staging

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.indication: Diagnostic and therapeutic — sphincterotomy, stone extraction, stent

imaging.name: EUS
imaging.indication: Detect small tumors, CBD stones, fine needle aspiration

imaging.name: PTC (Percutaneous Transhepatic Cholangiography)
imaging.indication: If ERCP fails — proximal/hilar obstruction, drains bile externally

resuscitation: Assess hydration status — IV Normal Saline if dehydrated
Correct coagulopathy: Vitamin K 10 mg IV slow push, repeat ×3 days
If INR still >1.5 and active bleeding: FFP 15 mL/kg
supportive: Pain control: paracetamol; avoid NSAIDs if cholestasis
Pruritus management: antihistamines (cetirizine), cholestyramine (4g QID), rifampicin
antibiotics: If cholangitis: Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole

surgical.indication: All cases of obstructive jaundice require biliary drainage
surgical.gallstone: ERCP + sphincterotomy + stone extraction → laparoscopic cholecystectomy
surgical.malignant_unresectable: Palliative biliary stenting (metal stent preferred) → chemotherapy
surgical.benign_stricture: ERCP + balloon dilatation + multiple stents → surgery if fail

nonOperative: ERCP + stent: plastic stent for benign/malignant, metal stent for malignant with >3 months life expectancy
PTC + external/internal drainage: for hilar obstruction or failed ERCP
EUS-guided drainage: emerging option for failed ERCP

general: Monitor drain output (if PTC or surgical drain)
LFTs daily — bilirubin trend confirms successful drainage
Bilirubin should drop by 30-50% within first week
discharge_criteria: Plan for definitive management (surgery or chemotherapy) confirmed

pathways.criteria: Gallstones in CBD, dilated ducts, no mass, fluctuating jaundice
pathways.treatment: ERCP + sphincterotomy + stone extraction. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy after clearance. IV antibiotics if cholangitis.

pathways.criteria: Mass on imaging, progressive jaundice, weight loss, Courvoisier sign
pathways.treatment: ERCP with biliary stent (plastic or metal). Staging CT/MRI. Pancreaticoduodenectomy if resectable. Palliative chemotherapy if unresectable.
IV antibiotics. Urgent ERCP with drainage. ICU if septic.

operative_templates.procedure: ERCP with Sphincterotomy and Stone Extraction
steps: Patient prone or left lateral under conscious sedation/GA
Pass duodenoscope to second part of duodenum
Cholangiogram — confirm stone(s) and ductal anatomy
Extract stones with Dormia basket or extraction balloon
If incomplete: place plastic stent (7-10 Fr) for drainage

watchFor: return of obstructive jaundice after initial improvement

dischargeCriteria: Bilirubin and ALP trending down significantly
No evidence of post-ERCP pancreatitis or bleeding

patient_education.explanation: Obstructive jaundice means bile is blocked from flowing from your liver to the intestine. The blockage can be caused by a gallstone, a narrowing (stricture), or a tumor. This causes bile to build up in the blood, turning your skin and eyes yellow. Treatment involves unblocking the bile duct, usually with an ERCP (endoscopic procedure) or surgery.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital urgently if: you develop fever with chills, the jaundice worsens, you have severe abdominal pain, you vomit blood or pass black stools, you feel confused or extremely drowsy, or you stop passing urine.
patient_education.activity: Rest during the acute phase. After ERCP or drainage, you can gradually resume normal activities. Avoid heavy lifting for 1-2 weeks after ERCP, 6 weeks after major surgery.
patient_education.diet: A low-fat diet is recommended. Avoid fried foods, fatty meats, and oily foods. Drink plenty of water. Small, frequent meals are better than large meals. If you have itching, avoid hot showers and wear loose cotton clothing.
patient_education.medication: Take Vitamin K as prescribed to correct your bleeding tendency. Take antibiotics if prescribed for cholangitis. Take antihistamines or cholestyramine for itching. Pain medication as needed. If you have a stent, you may need antibiotics before dental work.
patient_education.followUp: LFTs in 1-2 weeks to confirm improvement. If stone: arrange cholecystectomy. If stent: plan exchange in 3-6 months. If malignant: oncology referral for further management.

before_discharge: Plan definitive management (cholecystectomy, stent schedule, surgery)
Vitamin K and coagulopathy correction confirmed

after_discharge.action: Complete LFTs — bilirubin and ALP trend
Stent exchange/removal if plastic stent placed
Surveillance imaging and tumor markers if malignant
